2026-06-15.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Fatigue Resistance Intervals (Level 3)
2026-06-15.description:
2H45M @ 60-70% FTP, then 3-4x 2.5M intervals @ 98-103% FTP with 1.5M easy spinning between each interval for active recovery.

Finally cool down for remainder of ride @ 55-65% FTP.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. This session has the additional benefit of teaching your body to work hard in the presence of fatigue. These aerobic changes happen as a function of time rather than intensity, so achieving the target duration is most important.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The late-stage efforts also help develop aerobic abilities within higher-power muscle fibres, and improve your ability to ride at higher wattages in the presence of existing fatigue.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-06-15.title: Ramp Test (100W Start) |15W|60sec|15min warmup
2026-06-15.description:
https://zwifthacks.com/app/map-ftp-calculator/
STEPS
1. Set your FTP to 100W *IMPORTANT*

2. Start the workout and proceed through the 15W steps......

3. Record the following information
- Final Completed Stage (W):___________
- Uncompleted Stage (W):___________
- Duration of Uncompleted Stage:___________
Example:
- Final Completed Stage: 375W
- Uncompleted Stage: 400W
- Duration of Uncompleted Stage: 120seconds

4. Calculate your MAP with the figures
- 120 seconds completed / 150 seconds steps = 0.8
- 0.8 * 25W (increments) = 20W
- Final completed stage + % of uncompleted = 375+20 = 395W (MAP)

5. Calculate your estimated FTP
- 82.5% of MAP = 395W * 0.825 = 325.8W ESTIMATED FTP

6. Calculate your estimated 20minute FTP value
- 105% of your FTP = 325.8 * 1.05 = 342W

2026-06-01.title: Anaerobic Power: 30S Repeats (Level 3)
2026-06-01.description:
30M warm up, building up from 55-65% FTP towards 85-95% FTP, and then settling in to 55-75% FTP. Add in 2-3 10S surges at ~8/10 effort level to fully activate your muscles before the anaerobic efforts.

Then perform 10x 30S intervals @ a near maximal effort, paced consistently across the 30S interval (for most people this will be about 130-150% FTP, but for others it may be higher/lower). The first time you do this workout you may need to do these efforts based on feel, but in subsequent workouts you will have a better idea of what power you can target. The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Take 3M for active recovery @ 30-40% FTP (i.e. very easy spinning) after each interval. Take longer to recover if you feel you need to - the focus of this workout is to achieve maximal power in each interval, so the recovery can be as long as necessary to achieve this.

Remaining time up to ~1-1H30M @ 50-65% FTP to return body to resting levels steadily.

See workout details below for further details.

PURPOSE: The main effect of these intervals is to increase peak anaerobic power – that is the maximal rate at which energy can be produced anaerobically. This includes factors such as increased activity of enzymes associated with both the phosphocreatine system and the glycolytic system, and also increased glycogen storage within the muscles (Bangsbo et al., 2006).

In addition, these intervals can also contribute to improved anaerobic stamina through improved buffering of hydrogen ions (Bangsbo et al., 2006), and potential improvements in lactate transport (Pilegaard et al., 1999), aerobic capacity (Burgomaster et al., 2006) and mitochondrial function (Bishop et al., 2014, Tripp et al., 2022).

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

Bishop, D. J., Granata, C., & Eynon, N. (2014). Can we optimise the exercise training prescription to maximise improvements in mitochondria function and content?. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA)-General Subjects, 1840(4), 1266-1275.

Burgomaster, K. A., Heigenhauser, G. J., & Gibala, M. J. (2006). Effect of short-term sprint interval training on human skeletal muscle carbohydrate metabolism during exercise and time-trial performance. Journal of applied physiology, 100(6), 2041-2047.

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261.

Tripp, T. R., Frankish, B. P., Lun, V., Wiley, J. P., Shearer, J., Murphy, R. M., & MacInnis, M. J. (2022). Time course and fibre type‐dependent nature of calcium‐handling protein responses to sprint interval exercise in human skeletal muscle. The Journal of Physiology.

2026-06-01.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Neuromuscular Maintenance (Level 3)
2026-06-01.description:
Ride 2H50M @ 60-75% Max HR, keeping intensity in check on any hills.

Then include 4x 10S sprints @ 10/10 effort with 2M50S easy spinning between in final 30-40M of workout.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. This session has the additional benefit of teaching your body to work hard in the presence of fatigue and contributes to the maintenance of your neuromuscular capacity, alongside developing muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

You can read more about the science supporting the use of sprint training here:
https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/sms.14133
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/14986190/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23539308/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20424855/

Don't worry about straying out of the target intensity range for short periods on steeper climbs or descents - just try for as much time within target zone as possible. Try to choose as flat or rolling course if possible to maximise time in target zone.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:

2026-06-15.title: Endurance | Muscular Endurance (Level 2) | FTP
2026-06-15.description:
3h00 @ 60-70% FTP. In final 30-mins of ride, include 2x 8M blocks @ 70-85% FTP with 4M easy between blocks.

Keep intensity in check wherever possible, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The late-stage Zone 3 riding also helps develop aerobic abilities within higher-power muscle fibres, and improves your ability to ride at higher wattages in the presence of existing fatigue.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-06-15.title: Home-Based Circuit: General Strength Endurance
2026-06-15.description:
Repeat this circuit 3x through, completing each exercise until you can no longer hold good form. Once you can complete each exercise comfortably for more than 60-seconds or 20-reps (as applicable), the exercises can be progressed as described below. If you cannot do at least 6 reps or 10 seconds with good form, you need to use an easier level.

1. Single leg pistol squat onto chair
2. Plank.
3. Front lunge (step forward into lunge and then push explosively back up into standing position, alternating legs).
4. Side plank (each side)
5. Single leg calf raises with bent knee
6. Step up onto box/chair/step that is roughly knee height or slightly lower. Slowly lower back down in controlled manner. Try to drive motion using the leg that's on the step, using your trailing leg minimally.
7. Single leg calf raises with straight knee
8. Hip bridge

PROGRESSIONS:
1. Single leg pistol squat: Make this easier/harder by increasing/decreasing the height of the chair you sit onto (you can use books or cushions to do this). If you still cannot do this exercise with good form, then do some basic squats instead.

2. Plank: Make this harder by (i) lifting one arm (ii) lifting one leg (iii) alternating lifting alternate arms and legs or (iv) adding a wobble board beneath feet.

3. Front lunge: Make this harder using weight (e.g. kettlebell, weighted rucksack or vest)

4. Side plank: Make this harder by adding hip dips (i.e. raising and lowering your hips). As an additional progression, lift your top leg into the air while you do this.

5-7: Calf raises and step up: progress these by adding weight (e.g. kettlebell, weighted rucksack or vest).

8. Progress this by holding a static hip bridge on one leg. Can be further progressed by adding pulses of your hips, or by lowering up and down from the hip bridge position.

2026-06-15.title: OPTIONAL Recovery: Walk
2026-06-15.description:
SESSION: 30-120 minutes walking at a moderate to brisk pace, keeping heart rate between ~55-75% Threshold HR.

TERRAIN: Any.

PURPOSE: This session allows an opportunity for you to get outside, and loosen off your muscles and ligaments without adding any notable training stress or muscle damage. It facilitates recovery between harder training sessions.

It's worth noting that there’s surprisingly little scientific evidence to support or refute the use of a recovery walk in place of a full day off. The practice of doing a short recovery run/walk is nevertheless very common among athletes across a range of levels and disciplines. Personally, we find it's particularly good for loosening off the hip-flexors, which can get tight if you are predominantly desk-based at work. It also has a mental benefit in allowing an opportunity to get outside.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. The Zone 3 blocks also help develop aerobic abilities within and around higher-power muscle fibres, and may improve fatigue-resistance by developing muscular strength.

2026-06-01.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Mid-Ride Hill Repeats (Level 3)
2026-06-01.description:
3H @ 55-70% FTP, with 5x 2M hill repeats with each @ 105-121% FTP with 2M active recovery between each (1:1 work to rest ratio).

Keep intensity in check wherever possible during steady riding, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. This workout including the hill repeats will also start to prepare the leg muscles for higher intensity, longer duration intervals to come.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The mid-ride efforts also help induce fatigue and glycogen depletion early on in the ride, helping boost the stimulus for improved endurance and fat oxidation.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

By doing this session fasted, there's some evidence that aerobic adaptations and particularly capacity for fat oxidation may be improved. For more on this, check out the following papers:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21051570/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29315892/

2026-06-08.title: Type II Fat Oxidation: Fasted Shorter Low Cadence Efforts
2026-06-08.description:
Try to do this session fasted if possible (i.e. in the morning, before you've consumed any carbohydrates).

Start with ~30M warm up in the range of 45-70% FTP.

Then, complete 6x 5M @ 88-95% FTP with 3M recovery @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence during the intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres.

After this long set of tempo intervals, ride steadyfor remainder of ride @ 60-70% FTP.

PURPOSE: These low-cadence sweetspot intervals specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for improving fat oxidation in these fibres, and thus improving the lactate threshold.

These sessions also help to develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

By doing this session fasted, there's some evidence that aerobic adaptations and particularly capacity for fat oxidation may be improved. For more on this, check out the following papers:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21051570/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29315892/

PRE BIKE WARM UP
Banded Monster Walk 1x 15 steps
Banded Lateral Walk 1x 15 steps
Banded Glute Bridge 1x 15
Standing Hip Abduction with Band 1 x12 each side
Standing Hip Extension with Band 1x 12 each side
Toning Ball Rotation Reach 1x 10 each side
Walking Knee Hug 1x 10 each side

2026-06-08.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Muscular Endurance Blocks (Level 3)
2026-06-08.description:
3H10M @ 60-65% FTP including 10M muscular endurance blocks @ ~85-90% FTP every 30M throughout ride. Perform these blocks at a lower cadence (60-70RPM) to help emphasise the demand on the muscles for power production and lessen the involvement of the cardiovascular system.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarisation around the muscles, fatigue resistance of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary substrate.

The Zone 3 blocks also help develop aerobic abilities within and around higher-power muscle fibres, and may improve fatigue-resistance by developing muscular strength.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

Also helps to circulate oxygenated blood around the body and remove any metabolic waste products that may be lingering in the muscles from these previous workouts, since lactate is combusted as a fuel in the aerobic energy process better at a lower power output than it is when completely resting.

2026-06-15.title: Swim - RORY
2026-06-15.description: this is a technical session with specifics.

2026-06-15.title: Lactate Clearance: Over-Unders
2026-06-15.description:
Begin by warming up from 50% up to 70% for ~25M.

Then, go into over-under interval blocks, starting with a supra-threshold effort of 4M, before settling into a further 10M of undulations of 1M @ 104-106% FTP and 1.5M @ 70-80% FTP. Take ~4M recovery before repeating this over-under block once more.

Once complete, remaining time @ 60-70% FTP including some short 1M surges from 80-100% FTP to increase breathing rate and provide more interest than a solid steady block! Cool down in final 10M or so @ an easy effort level.

PURPOSE: The lactate threshold is a result of the balance between aerobic energy system strength and your anaerobic energy system strength. This means, it is a product of your VO2max (your maximal ability to take in, deliver and use oxygen for energy production), your VLamax (maximal ability to produce lactate, which is a byproduct of being able to combust more carbohydrate in the form of glycogen) and then the clearance of accumulated lactate when the aerobic system is saturated with pyruvate and cannot process any more lactate/pyruvate. Thus, training to improve the lactate threshold (fractional utilisation of the VO2) is building as strong a VO2max as possible, an appropriately strong VLamax (where appropriate could mean a very low VLamax depending on the demands of competition) and the ability to shuttle the excess lactate away. This workout targets the latter, by accumulating lactate during the over-threshold portions of each block, and then training the body to clear them by dropping just below the lactate threshold for short periods.

These intervals work on the principle that maximal rates of lactate clearance typically occur around 80-90% FTP. To read more on this check out these papers:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20544484/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24739289/

In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

By doing this session fasted, there's some evidence that aerobic adaptations and particularly capacity for fat oxidation may be improved. For more on this, check out the following papers:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21051570/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29315892/

2026-06-22.title: Type II Fat Oxidation: Fasted 10M Blocks + Threshold Finishes
2026-06-22.description:
Try to do this session fasted if possible (i.e. in the morning, before you've consumed any carbohydrates).

1H30M ~60-70% FTP with 10M tempo blocks consisting of 8M @ 82-87% FTP and a finish of 2M @ 95-100% FTP. Use a low cadence during the first 8M of these intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres.

Feel free to alter cadence throughout workout, as well as pedalling style (seated and standing).

PURPOSE: These low-cadence tempo intervals specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for improving fat oxidation in these fibres, and thus improving the lactate threshold.

These sessions also help to develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

By doing this session fasted, there's some evidence that aerobic adaptations and particularly capacity for fat oxidation may be improved. For more on this, check out the following papers:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21051570/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29315892/

2026-06-22.title: Endurance: Negative Split Ride
2026-06-22.description:
Steady ride of around 2.15H with first 1H30M @ Zone 2 (60-70% FTP) and last 45M @ Zone 3 (70-85% FTP).

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. In addition, increases muscle's ability to resist fatigue (muscular endurance). Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance, and reduced VLamax (maximal lactate building rate).

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

Keep intensity in check wherever possible during steady riding, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. This workout including the hill repeats will also start to prepare the leg muscles for higher intensity, longer duration intervals to come.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The mid-ride efforts also help induce fatigue and glycogen depletion early on in the ride, helping boost the stimulus for improved endurance and fat oxidation.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:

2026-06-01.title: Anaerobic Stamina: 90S Repeats (Level 3)
2026-06-01.description:
30M warm-up beginning with a ~12M ramp up from around 55-65% Max HR to 85-95% Max HR. Then settle into @ 60-75% Max HR, including 2-3 10S surges at a 8-9/10 intensity to fully activate muscles before anaerobic efforts.

Then, perform 2x lots of 4x 90S @ 9/10 effort during work intervals, taking around 2M for active recovery (i.e. very easy spinning), with a ~15M block between each set @ 50-60% Max HR. The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Remaining time @ 60-70% Max HR to return body to resting levels steadily.

PURPOSE: Develops ability to produce high amounts of power well above maximal oxygen uptake levels (VO2Max) and promotes recruitment of Type II (fast twitch) muscles in power production. This session, with its relatively short recoveries, works in particular on your ability to sustain anaerobic energy production for longer periods or across repeated bursts. Applied during short, sharp climbs or for attacks within races.

This session draws on research that has shown an improvements in anaerobic capacity and lactate shuttling and buffering as a result of intervals similar to this design. Examples include:

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261

Edge, J., Bishop, D., & Goodman, C. (2006). The effects of training intensity on muscle buffer capacity in females. European journal of applied physiology, 96(1), 97-105.

Weston, A. R., Myburgh, K. H., Lindsay, F. H., Dennis, S. C., Noakes, T. D., & Hawley, J. A. (1996). Skeletal muscle buffering capacity and endurance performance after high-intensity interval training by well-trained cyclists. European journal of applied physiology and occupational physiology, 75(1), 7-13.

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The late-stage efforts also help develop aerobic abilities within higher-power muscle fibres, and improve your ability to ride at higher wattages in the presence of existing fatigue.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-06-22.title: Anaerobic Power: 30S Repeats (Level 2)
2026-06-22.description:
30M warm-up beginning with a ~8M ramp up from around 55-65% Max HR to 85-95% Max HR. Then settle into @ 60-75% Max HR, including 2-3 10S surges at a 8-9/10 intensity to fully activate muscles before anaerobic efforts.

Then perform 8x 30S intervals @ a near maximal effort, paced consistently across the 30S interval (HR targets within workout details are for illustrative purposes only - your HR will not reach max). The goal is to hit a power that you can hold consistently across each of the intervals, so don't go so hard on the first one that you have nothing left for the last ones.

Take 3M for active recovery (i.e. very easy spinning) after each interval.   Take longer to recover if you feel you need to - the focus of this workout is to achieve maximal power in each interval, so the recovery can be as long as necessary to achieve this.

Remaining time @ 60-70% Max HR to return body to resting levels steadily.

See workout details below for further details.

PURPOSE: The main effect of these intervals is to increase peak anaerobic power – that is the maximal rate at which energy can be produced anaerobically. This includes factors such as increased activity of enzymes associated with both the phosphocreatine system and the glycolytic system, and also increased glycogen storage within the muscles (Bangsbo et al., 2006).

In addition, these intervals can also contribute to improved anaerobic stamina through improved buffering of hydrogen ions (Bangsbo et al., 2006), and potential improvements in lactate transport (Pilegaard et al., 1999), aerobic capacity (Burgomaster et al., 2006) and mitochondrial function (Bishop et al., 2014, Tripp et al., 2022).

Bangsbo, J., Mohr, M., Poulsen, A., Perez-Gomez, J., & Krustrup, P. (2006). Training and testing the elite athlete. J Exerc Sci Fit, 4(1), 1-14.

Bishop, D. J., Granata, C., & Eynon, N. (2014). Can we optimise the exercise training prescription to maximise improvements in mitochondria function and content?. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA)-General Subjects, 1840(4), 1266-1275.

Burgomaster, K. A., Heigenhauser, G. J., & Gibala, M. J. (2006). Effect of short-term sprint interval training on human skeletal muscle carbohydrate metabolism during exercise and time-trial performance. Journal of applied physiology, 100(6), 2041-2047.

Pilegaard, H., Domino, K., Noland, T., Juel, C., Hellsten, Y., Halestrap, A. P., & Bangsbo, J. (1999). Effect of high-intensity exercise training on lactate/H+ transport capacity in human skeletal muscle. American Journal of Physiology-Endocrinology And Metabolism, 276(2), E255-E261.

Tripp, T. R., Frankish, B. P., Lun, V., Wiley, J. P., Shearer, J., Murphy, R. M., & MacInnis, M. J. (2022). Time course and fibre type‐dependent nature of calcium‐handling protein responses to sprint interval exercise in human skeletal muscle. The Journal of Physiology.

2026-06-08.title: Strength & Conditioning: Home-Based Circuit (All Levels)
2026-06-08.description:
Repeat this circuit 3x through, completing each exercise until you can no longer hold good form. Once you can complete each exercise comfortably for more than 60-seconds or 20-reps (as applicable), the exercises can be progressed as described below. If you cannot do at least 6 reps or 10 seconds with good form, you need to use an easier level.

1. Single leg pistol squat onto chair
2. Plank.
3. Front lunge (step forward into lunge and then push explosively back up into standing position, alternating legs).
4. Side plank (each side)
5. Single leg calf raises with bent knee
6. Step up onto box/chair/step that is roughly knee height or slightly lower. Slowly lower back down in controlled manner. Try to drive motion using the leg that's on the step, using your trailing leg minimally.
7. Single leg calf raises with straight knee
8. Hip bridge

PROGRESSIONS:
1. Single leg pistol squat: Make this easier/harder by increasing/decreasing the height of the chair you sit onto (you can use books or cushions to do this). If you still cannot do this exercise with good form, then do some basic squats instead.

2. Plank: Make this harder by (i) lifting one arm (ii) lifting one leg (iii) alternating lifting alternate arms and legs or (iv) adding a wobble board beneath feet.

3. Front lunge: Make this harder using weight (e.g. kettlebell, weighted rucksack or vest)

4. Side plank: Make this harder by adding hip dips (i.e. raising and lowering your hips). As an additional progression, lift your top leg into the air while you do this.

5-7: Calf raises and step up: progress these by adding weight (e.g. kettlebell, weighted rucksack or vest).

8. Progress this by holding a static hip bridge on one leg. Can be further progressed by adding pulses of your hips, or by lowering up and down from the hip bridge position.

PURPOSE: This session is designed to improve strength endurance, and to address any left/right imbalances in strength, which contributes to improved running form, economy and reduced injury risk.

8. Hip bridge

PROGRESSIONS:
1. Single leg pistol squat: Make this easier/harder by increasing/decreasing the height of the chair you sit onto (you can use books or cushions to do this). If you still cannot do this exercise with good form, then do some basic squats instead.

2. Plank: Make this harder by (i) lifting one arm (ii) lifting one leg (iii) alternating lifting alternate arms and legs or (iv) adding a wobble board beneath feet.

3. Front lunge: Make this harder using weight (e.g. kettlebell, weighted rucksack or vest)

4. Side plank: Make this harder by adding hip dips (i.e. raising and lowering your hips). As an additional progression, lift your top leg into the air while you do this.

5-7: Calf raises and step up: progress these by adding weight (e.g. kettlebell, weighted rucksack or vest).

8. Progress this by holding a static hip bridge on one leg. Can be further progressed by adding pulses of your hips, or by lowering up and down from the hip bridge position.

PURPOSE: This session is designed to improve strength endurance, and to address any left/right imbalances in strength, which contributes to improved running form, economy and reduced injury risk.

2026-06-08.title: Muscular Endurance: Medium Low Cadence Efforts (Level 1)
2026-06-08.description:
Start with ~20M warm up in the range of 45-65% FTP.

Then complete 4x 7.5M @ 85-92% FTP and 2.5M @ 50-60% FTP. Use a low cadence for muscular endurance intervals of ~60-75RPM to increase force demands on muscle fibres. This session could be done as a set of hill repeats.

After this long set of tempo intervals, cool down for remainder of ride @ 50-60% FTP.

PURPOSE: To develop greater fatigue resistance or muscular endurance through sustained work that places higher force demand on the muscle fibres. In doing so, you train the muscle fibres to become more resistance to micro tears and general damage. When muscle fibres (especially slow twitch or Type I fibres) become damaged because of a relatively high sustained workloads, they have to call upon the far less aerobically-efficient Type II fibres to help maintain a consistent power output an carry the load. This has the effect of reducing what's called your 'exercise economy/efficiency', which essentially means you need to supply more oxygen (and fuel) for the same power output (very similar to the fuel efficiency of a car). This is one of the reasons you can see heart rate drift for a given power as you become fatigued in a race or long training session, since the heart is required to pump more oxygen to sustain the workload once the muscles become fatigued. By improving your exercise economy via greater resistance to fatigue/damage, you're able to improve your endurance at a broad range of intensities.

These low-cadence tempo intervals also specifically target aerobic adaptations within Type IIa muscle fibres, which typically aren't substantially activated in a lower intensity endurance ride until the latter stages of the ride. These intervals are good for reducing VLamax, and thus improving lactate threshold.

2026-06-08.title: Threshold: 4x 4min (Level 1) | FTP
2026-06-08.description:
20-30min warm up building up to 50-70% FTP.

4x 4min @ 103-108% FTP with 3min active recovery between each interval. Best done on shallow grade climb if riding outdoors, standing on the pedals for most of the intervals.

Remaining time @ ~50-60% FTP - can cool down longer to 90min if time allows.

PURPOSE: Improve muscular endurance and lactate threshold, as well as improve body's ability to deliver large amounts of oxygen to working muscles.

These intervals work on the principle of the 'VO2 slow component' (see e.g. here: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21552162/). At intensities between the lactate threshold and the maximal aerobic power, oxygen intake gradually rises towards VO2max.

These intervals are also inspired by the following study, which shows supra-threshold intervals to be more tolerable than classic VO2max intervals: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28051345/

While we’re not aware of any scientific research supporting this, we also believe these supra-threshold intervals likely contribute to improved lactate shuttling and buffering. Theoretically, this makes sense as lactate levels are held at a high level throughout the intervals, placing stress on the lactate shuttling and buffering systems.

2026-06-15.title: Endurance | Muscular Endurance (Level 2) | FTP
2026-06-15.description:
3h00 @ 60-70% FTP. In final 30-mins of ride, include 2x 8M blocks @ 70-85% FTP with 4M easy between blocks.

Keep intensity in check wherever possible, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The late-stage Zone 3 riding also helps develop aerobic abilities within higher-power muscle fibres, and improves your ability to ride at higher wattages in the presence of existing fatigue.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-06-01.title: Ramp Test (100W Start) |15W|60sec|15min warmup
2026-06-01.description:
https://zwifthacks.com/app/map-ftp-calculator/
STEPS
1. Set your FTP to 100W *IMPORTANT*

2. Start the workout and proceed through the 15W steps......

3. Record the following information
- Final Completed Stage (W):___________
- Uncompleted Stage (W):___________
- Duration of Uncompleted Stage:___________
Example:
- Final Completed Stage: 375W
- Uncompleted Stage: 400W
- Duration of Uncompleted Stage: 120seconds

4. Calculate your MAP with the figures
- 120 seconds completed / 150 seconds steps = 0.8
- 0.8 * 25W (increments) = 20W
- Final completed stage + % of uncompleted = 375+20 = 395W (MAP)

5. Calculate your estimated FTP
- 82.5% of MAP = 395W * 0.825 = 325.8W ESTIMATED FTP

6. Calculate your estimated 20minute FTP value
- 105% of your FTP = 325.8 * 1.05 = 342W

2026-06-01.title: Endurance: Steady Ride + Mid-Ride Hill Repeats (Level 3)
2026-06-01.description:
3H @ 55-70% FTP, with 5x 2M hill repeats with each @ 105-110% FTP with 2M active recovery between each (1:1 work to rest ratio).

Keep intensity in check wherever possible during steady riding, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Enhances aerobic fitness by stimulating greater mitochondrial biogenesis (production of mitochondria - the power house of the cell), enhanced capillarization in the muscles, development of slow-twitch muscles fibres and trains body to more efficiently use fat as primary fuel source. This workout including the hill repeats will also start to prepare the leg muscles for higher intensity, longer duration intervals to come.

Ultimately, these endurance rides contribute to improved aerobic capacity, lactate threshold and muscular endurance.

The mid-ride efforts also help induce fatigue and glycogen depletion early on in the ride, helping boost the stimulus for improved endurance and fat oxidation.

You can read more about the importance of low-intensity training such as this in this great article by Stephen Seiler and Espen Tønnessen:
https://sportsci.org/2009/ss.htm

2026-06-15.title: VO2Max: 30S/15S Microbursts (Level 1)
2026-06-15.description:
20M warm up building up to 65-75% Max HR. Include a 1M effort at 8/10 effort level to fully activate muscles before main set of intervals.

3x 9M (30S @ 9/10 effort/15S easy) with 3-4M easy spinning as active recovery between each set of micro intervals.

Remaining time @ 60-75% Max HR to cool down.

PURPOSE: Increase the maximal volume of oxygen the body can take in and deliver to the working muscles. Stimulates improvements in stroke volume and mitochondrial function. This particular workout design also improves speed of recovery between hard efforts (lactate buffering), enhances the ability to overcome force due to the need to re-accelerate multiple times, and will also serve to increase anaerobic capacity and VLamax, given that a large amount of lactate can accumulate during the course of each block of micro-intervals.

The seminal study investigating the benefits of this style of microburst intervals and to observe a beneficial effect on aerobic and anaerobic capacity is this study by Tabata et al.:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8897392/

More recent research by Rønnestad et al. suggests that microburst intervals might out-perform the classic-style VO2max intervals in developing aerobic capacity:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24382021/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31977120/

2026-05-25.title: Endurance TEST: Long Steady Ride + Muscular TEST (Level 2, Level 3)
2026-05-25.description:
4H @ 60-70% FTP. During the ride do 5x 1min hard efforts to accumulate lactate. Clear it out then do 10M tempo, clear that out and fuel before starting the 1x 20M block @ best effort % FTP.

Dont start too hard, the 10min should gauge your level of readiness. Keep intensity in check wherever possible, but don't stress straying into Zone 1 on downhills or Zone 3 on steeper climbs.

PURPOSE: Test the outdoor power during a outdoor ride on a climb.

The late-stage Zone 3 riding also helps develop aerobic abilities within higher-power muscle fibres, and improves your ability to ride at higher wattages in the presence of existing fatigue.

Created by DBT coaching.
